sentences.base_language: I go to the market in the morning.
sentences.grammarNote: أَذهَب (adhhab, I go) — Form I of root ذ-ه-ب (dh-h-b, to go). The present-tense first-person prefix is أَ (a-). السُّوق (as-sūq, the market) — a sun letter word where الـ assimilates: al-sūq → as-sūq. السُّوق is masculine in MSA. فِي الصَّبَاح (fī aṣ-ṣabāḥ, in the morning) — a prepositional time phrase; الصَّبَاح from root ص-ب-ح (ṣ-b-ḥ, morning/dawn).

sentences.base_language: The market is busy and full of people.
sentences.grammarNote: مُزدَحِم (muzdaḥim, crowded) — Form VIII active participle of ز-ح-م (z-ḥ-m, to crowd): اِزدَحَمَ = to be crowded. مَلِيء بِالنَّاس (malīʾ bin-nās, full of people) — مَلِيء (full, فَعِيل pattern) from root م-ل-أ (m-l-ʾ, to fill). The preposition بِـ here means 'with/of'. الناس (an-nās, the people) is a collective noun with no singular — always treated as plural.

sentences.base_language: I need tomatoes, onions, and oranges.
sentences.grammarNote: أَحتَاج (aḥtāj, I need) — Form VIII of ح-و-ج (ḥ-w-j, need): اِحتَاجَ = to need. Unlike عَندي (I have), this verb can take a direct object directly without إِلى. طَمَاطِم (ṭamāṭim, tomatoes) and بَصَل (baṣal, onion) are collective nouns — they refer to the food type as a whole. بُرتُقَال (burtuqāl, oranges) is also collective; a single orange is بُرتُقَالَة.

sentences.base_language: I stop at the vegetable seller.
sentences.grammarNote: أَتَوَقَّف (atawwaqqaf, I stop) — Form V of و-ق-ف (w-q-f, to stand): تَوَقَّفَ = to come to a stop (reflexive/intensive). بَائِع الخُضَار (bāʾiʿ al-khuḍḍār, vegetable seller) — iḍāfa: بَائِع (seller, فَاعِل active participle of ب-ي-ع, to sell) + الخُضَار (the vegetables, collective noun from root خ-ض-ر, green). عِند (ʿind, at/near) is a preposition of location.
بكم (bikam) = how much? It is formed from بـ (bi, for/at) + كم (kam, how much/how many). This single short phrase is one of the most useful in any market — no verb needed.

ثَلَاثَة (thalātha, three) — Arabic numbers from 3–10 show gender polarity: the number takes the opposite gender from the noun it counts. دَرَاهِم (darāhim, dirhams) is masculine, so the number takes the feminine ة. This reversal of gender (called عَكس al-ʿaks) is one of Arabic's most distinctive features. لِلكِيلُو (lil-kīlū, per kilogram) — لـ + الكيلو.

sentences.base_language: That is expensive! Can you give me a better price?
sentences.target_language: هذا غالٍ! هل يمكنك أن تعطيني سعراً أفضل؟
sentences.romanized: hadha ghalin! hal yumkinuka an tu'tiyani si'ran afdal?
sentences.literal: This expensive! Is possible-for-you that you-give-me price better?
sentences.grammarNote: غالٍ (ghalin) ends in ـنٍ — a nunation on a defective adjective. The noun form غالي loses its final ي before case endings in certain positions. You will see this shape frequently with adjectives and nouns that end in a long vowel.

sentences.base_language: OK, two and a half dirhams. Final price.
sentences.grammarNote: دِرهَمَان (dirhamān, two dirhams) — the dual nominative form: ـَان (-ān) suffix. In a genitive or accusative context it becomes دِرهَمَين (-ayn). وَنِصف (wa-niṣf, and a half) — نِصف (half) from root ن-ص-ف (n-ṣ-f, to halve). حَسَناً (ḥasanan, OK/good) — indefinite accusative of حَسَن (good), used as an interjection; the tanwin ـاً signals it is a frozen adverbial form.

sentences.base_language: I will take two kilos of tomatoes and one kilo of onions.
sentences.grammarNote: سَ (sa-, near future prefix) attached to the verb: سَآخُذ (sa-ākhudh, I will take). This is distinct from سَوفَ (sawfa), which implies a more distant or uncertain future. كِيلُوَين (kīlwayn, two kilos) — dual of كِيلُو: the dual suffix ـَين expresses exactly two, eliminating the need for the number word اثنان. أَخَذَ (akhadha, to take) from root أ-خ-ذ (ʾ-kh-dh).

sentences.base_language: And a bag of oranges too, please.
sentences.grammarNote: كِيس بُرتُقَال (kīs burtuqāl, a bag of oranges) — iḍāfa: كِيس (bag/sack, from root ك-ي-س) + بُرتُقَال (oranges, indefinite collective). أَيضاً (ayḍan, also/too) — an adverb from root أ-ي-ض; used after the item it modifies. مِن فَضلِك (min faḍlika, please) — literally 'from your grace/favour'; a universally polite closing in requests.

تفضل (tafaddal) is a versatile word meaning 'here you go', 'please come in', 'help yourself', or 'go ahead'. It comes from the root فضل (fadl, grace/favour). The feminine form is تفضلي (tafaddali) and the plural is تفضلوا (tafaddalu).
